docs.title: TorcPy
docs.text:
TorcPy is a distributed workflow orchestration system for Python. It manages complex computational pipelines with job dependencies, resource tracking, and parallel local execution — all backed by a lightweight SQLite database and a clean REST API.

flowchart LR
    subgraph input [" "]
        A[Prepare Data]
    end

    subgraph parallel [" "]
        direction TB
        B1[Process 1]
        B2[Process 2]
        B3[Process 3]
        B4[Process 4]
    end

    subgraph output [" "]
        C[Aggregate Results]
    end

    A --> B1 & B2 & B3 & B4
    B1 & B2 & B3 & B4 --> C

    style A fill:#3f51b5,stroke:#283593,color:#fff
    style B1 fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#fff
    style B2 fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#fff
    style B3 fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#fff
    style B4 fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#fff
    style C fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#4a148c,color:#fff
    style input fill:none,stroke:none
    style parallel fill:none,stroke:none
    style output fill:none,stroke:none

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Declarative Workflow Definitions — Define workflows in YAML or JSON. Dependencies are inferred automatically from file and data relationships.
  • Parameter Expansion — Turn one job definition into hundreds with simple syntax: "1:100", "[0.001, 0.01, 0.1]", Cartesian product or zip.
  • Resource-Aware Scheduling — Track CPU, memory, and GPU availability. Jobs only run when resources are available.
  • Deferred Dependency Unblocking — Background task processes job completions and unblocks dependents for scalable pipelines.
  • REST API + Python Client — Full HTTP API with a typed async Python client library.
  • Rich CLI — Beautiful terminal output powered by rich-click.
  • Pure Python — No Rust, no compiled extensions. Install with uv or pip.

docs.title: Who Should Use TorcPy?
docs.text:
• Data Scientists running parameter sweeps and model training pipelines
  • Computational Researchers with multi-stage simulation workflows
  • ML Engineers orchestrating preprocessing → training → evaluation pipelines
  • Anyone who needs reliable, resumable job orchestration without HPC cluster complexity

docs.title: Documentation Structure
docs.text:
Core (for all users):

  • Getting Started — Install and run your first workflow in minutes
  • Concepts — Architecture, job states, and dependency resolution
  • Tutorials — Step-by-step workflow patterns
  • How-To Guides — Targeted solutions to specific tasks
  • Monitoring — Reports, logs, and debugging
  • Reference — CLI, spec format, API, environment variables

Advanced:

  • Design — Internal architecture for contributors

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text:
pipeline.yaml
name: ml-pipeline

files:
  - name: raw_data
    path: data/raw.csv
    st_mtime: 1710000000.0   # marks as pre-existing input
  - name: features
    path: data/features.csv
  - name: model
    path: models/model.pkl

jobs:
  - name: extract_features
    command: python extract.py --input data/raw.csv --output data/features.csv
    input_files: [raw_data]
    output_files: [features]

  - name: train_model
    command: python train.py --features data/features.csv --output models/model.pkl
    input_files: [features]
    output_files: [model]
    resource_requirements:
      num_cpus: 8
      memory: "16g"
      runtime: "PT2H"

  - name: evaluate
    command: python evaluate.py --model models/model.pkl
    depends_on: [train_model]

$ torcpy server run --db pipeline.db &
$ torcpy run pipeline.yaml
Created workflow 1
Initialized: 1 ready, 2 blocked
Running job 1: extract_features
Running job 2: train_model
Running job 3: evaluate

Workflow 1 finished:
  Completed: 3
  Failed:    0

docs.title: Core Concepts
docs.text:
Understand how TorcPy works before building complex workflows.

Concept Description Architecture Client-server design, components, data flow Job States The 11 job statuses and valid transitions Dependency Resolution Explicit deps, implicit file/data deps, unblocking Workflow Specification The YAML/JSON spec format Parameter Expansion Ranges, lists, Cartesian product, zip Resource Management CPU, memory, GPU tracking and scheduling

docs.title: Architecture Overview
docs.text: TorcPy uses a client-server architecture where a lightweight REST API server manages all workflow state, and one or more worker processes claim and execute jobs.

docs.title: Components
docs.text:
flowchart TB
    subgraph server ["Server Process"]
        API["FastAPI REST API"]
        BG["Background Unblock Task"]
        DB[("SQLite Database")]
        API --> DB
        BG --> DB
    end

    subgraph workers ["Worker Processes"]
        W1["Job Runner 1"]
        W2["Job Runner 2"]
    end

    CLI["CLI / Python Client"]

    CLI -- "HTTP" --> API
    W1 -- "claim_next_jobs\ncomplete_job" --> API
    W2 -- "claim_next_jobs\ncomplete_job" --> API

docs.title: Server
docs.text:
The server is a FastAPI application backed by a SQLite database. It exposes a REST API for all workflow, job, file, and resource management operations.

Key design decisions:

  • WAL mode — SQLite runs in Write-Ahead Logging mode for concurrent reads.
  • BEGIN IMMEDIATE — Job claiming uses an immediate write lock, preventing two workers from claiming the same job.
  • Foreign key cascades — Deleting a workflow automatically removes all associated jobs, files, results, etc.
  • Background unblock task — A background asyncio.Task processes completed jobs and transitions blocked dependents to ready. This is deliberately not done inline for performance.

docs.title: Workers
docs.text:
Workers are Python processes that run torcpy workflows run <id>. Each worker:

  1. Polls the server for ready jobs
  2. Checks local resource availability (CPU, memory, GPU)
  3. Claims a batch of jobs via POST /workflows/{id}/jobs/claim
  4. Executes each job as an asyncio.subprocess
  5. Reports results via POST /workflows/{id}/results
  6. Completes the job via POST /workflows/{id}/jobs/{job_id}/complete

docs.title: CLI / Python Client
docs.text: The torcpy command and TorcClient Python class are thin HTTP wrappers over the server API. They share the same httpx-based async client.

docs.title: Data Flow
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant CLI
    participant Server
    participant Worker

    User->>CLI: torcpy run pipeline.yaml
    CLI->>Server: POST /workflows (create)
    CLI->>Server: POST /workflows/{id}/files (bulk)
    CLI->>Server: POST /workflows/{id}/jobs (bulk)
    CLI->>Server: POST /workflows/{id}/initialize
    Server->>Server: Build dependency graph
    Server->>Server: Mark ready jobs

    loop Poll until done
        Worker->>Server: POST /jobs/claim?count=5
        Server->>Worker: [job1, job2, ...]
        Worker->>Worker: Execute jobs in parallel
        Worker->>Server: POST /results (metrics)
        Worker->>Server: POST /jobs/{id}/complete?status=5
        Server->>Server: Background: unblock dependents
    end

    CLI->>User: Workflow finished

docs.title: Database Schema (Simplified)
docs.text:
erDiagram
    workflow ||--o{ job : contains
    workflow ||--o{ file : contains
    workflow ||--o{ user_data : contains
    workflow ||--o{ result : has
    job ||--o{ job_depends_on : "depends on"
    job ||--o{ job_input_file : reads
    job ||--o{ job_output_file : writes
    job }o--|| resource_requirements : uses
    job }o--|| failure_handler : uses
    job ||--o{ result : produces

    workflow {
        int id PK
        string name
        string user
        float timestamp
        json metadata
    }
    job {
        int id PK
        int workflow_id FK
        string name
        string command
        int status
        int priority
        int unblocking_processed
    }
    result {
        int id PK
        int job_id FK
        int return_code
        float exec_time_minutes
        int peak_memory_bytes
    }

docs.title: Dependency Resolution
docs.text: TorcPy supports two types of job dependencies: explicit (declared in the spec) and implicit (inferred from file and user-data relationships).

docs.title: Explicit Dependencies
docs.text:
Declare dependencies directly in the workflow spec using job names:

jobs:
  - name: step_a
    command: echo A

  - name: step_b
    command: echo B
    depends_on: [step_a]   # step_b waits for step_a

  - name: step_c
    command: echo C
    depends_on: [step_a, step_b]   # step_c waits for both

docs.title: Regex Dependencies
docs.text:
You can also match dependencies by regex pattern. This is useful for fan-in after parameterized jobs:

jobs:
  - name: train_{i}
    command: python train.py --fold {i}
    parameters:
      i: "1:5"

  - name: aggregate
    command: python aggregate.py
    depends_on_regexes:
      - "train_.*"     # waits for all 5 train jobs

docs.title: Implicit Dependencies (via Files)
docs.text:
When job A writes a file and job B reads that same file, TorcPy automatically creates a dependency: B depends on A.

files:
  - name: raw_data
    path: data/input.csv
    st_mtime: 1710000000.0   # pre-existing input — no job writes this

  - name: features
    path: data/features.csv   # generated by preprocess

jobs:
  - name: preprocess
    command: python preprocess.py
    input_files: [raw_data]
    output_files: [features]

  - name: train
    command: python train.py
    input_files: [features]   # implicit dep on preprocess (writes features)

The dependency graph becomes:

flowchart LR
    pre[preprocess] --> train[train]
    style pre fill:#3f51b5,color:#fff
    style train fill:#2e7d32,color:#fff

docs.title: The st_mtime Field
docs.text:
The st_mtime field on a FileSpec distinguishes input files from output files:

  • Set (st_mtime: <timestamp>) → pre-existing input file. No job needs to produce it.
  • Not set (st_mtime: null) → output file. Some job must write it.

Only files with no st_mtime participate in implicit dependency creation.

docs.title: Implicit Dependencies (via User Data)
docs.text:
The same logic applies to user_data items:

user_data:
  - name: config
    data: {learning_rate: 0.001}

  - name: best_params
    is_ephemeral: false

jobs:
  - name: tune
    command: python tune.py
    input_user_data: [config]
    output_user_data: [best_params]

  - name: train_final
    command: python train_final.py
    input_user_data: [best_params]   # implicit dep on tune

docs.title: Initialization
docs.text:
Dependencies are built when you call initialize:

torcpy workflows initialize <id>

Or automatically when running:

torcpy run <spec_or_id>
torcpy workflows run <id>

During initialization:

  1. Implicit dependencies are inserted into job_depends_on (from file/user_data relationships)
  2. Jobs with no dependencies → ready (2)
  3. Jobs with unmet dependencies → blocked (1)

docs.title: Circular Dependency Detection
docs.text:
Warning

TorcPy does not currently detect circular dependencies at initialization time. A circular dependency will result in all involved jobs remaining blocked indefinitely. Ensure your workflow graph is a DAG (directed acyclic graph).

docs.title: Background Unblocking
docs.text:
When a job completes, TorcPy does not immediately check and unblock dependents. Instead:

  1. The complete_job endpoint sets unblocking_processed = 0 on the completed job.
  2. A background asyncio.Task runs on a 1-second interval.
  3. The background task finds all completed jobs with unblocking_processed = 0.
  4. For each, it checks whether all dependencies of each blocked dependent are now complete.
  5. Satisfied dependents are transitioned to ready.

This design ensures job completion endpoints remain fast even in large workflows.

docs.title: Job States
docs.text: Every job in TorcPy has a status stored as an integer in the database. Understanding these states is essential for debugging and monitoring workflows.

docs.title: State Reference
docs.text: Value Name Description 0 uninitialized Newly created, not yet part of the dependency graph 1 blocked Waiting for one or more dependencies to complete 2 ready All dependencies satisfied, available to be claimed 3 pending Claimed by a worker, queued for execution 4 running Actively executing as a subprocess 5 completed Finished with return code 0 6 failed Finished with non-zero return code 7 canceled Canceled by user or due to a blocking job failure 8 terminated Forcibly killed (e.g., walltime exceeded) 9 disabled Excluded from this workflow run 10 pending_failed Failure handler could not determine recovery action

docs.title: State Transition Diagram
docs.text:
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> uninitialized : job created

    uninitialized --> blocked : initialize (has deps)
    uninitialized --> ready : initialize (no deps)

    blocked --> ready : all deps completed\n(background task)
    blocked --> canceled : blocking dep failed\n+ cancel_on_blocking_job_failure

    ready --> pending : worker claims job
    pending --> running : worker starts execution
    running --> completed : exit code 0
    running --> failed : exit code != 0
    running --> terminated : walltime exceeded

    completed --> uninitialized : reset
    failed --> uninitialized : reset
    canceled --> uninitialized : reset

    failed --> ready : failure handler retries
    failed --> pending_failed : no matching handler

docs.title: Terminal States
docs.text:
Jobs in these states will not be retried unless explicitly reset:

  • completed — Success
  • failed — Execution failure
  • canceled — Canceled
  • terminated — Killed
  • disabled — Excluded
  • pending_failed — Unhandled failure

docs.title: The unblocking_processed Flag
docs.text:
When a job transitions to a terminal state (completed, failed, canceled, terminated), TorcPy sets unblocking_processed = 0. The background task watches for this flag and:

  1. For completed jobs: checks each blocked dependent — if all its dependencies are now complete, transitions it to ready.
  2. For failed/canceled/terminated jobs: if a dependent has cancel_on_blocking_job_failure = true, it is transitioned to canceled.

After processing, unblocking_processed is set back to 1.

Do not complete jobs via update

Always use the POST /jobs/{id}/complete endpoint (or client.complete_job()) to finish a job. This endpoint sets unblocking_processed = 0 and signals the background task. Using PATCH /jobs/{id} to change status bypasses this mechanism.

docs.title: Filtering Jobs by Status
docs.text:
# List only ready jobs
torcpy jobs list 1 --status 2

# List only failed jobs
torcpy jobs list 1 --status 6

In the Python client:

from torcpy.models.enums import JobStatus

jobs = await client.list_jobs(workflow_id=1, status=JobStatus.FAILED)

docs.title: Parameter Expansion
docs.text: TorcPy can expand a single job (or file) definition into many instances using the parameters field. This is ideal for parameter sweeps, cross-validation, and multi-dataset pipelines.

docs.title: Syntax Overview
docs.text:
Add a parameters dict to any job or file spec:

jobs:
  - name: train_{fold}
    command: python train.py --fold {fold}
    parameters:
      fold: "1:5"     # expands to fold=1, 2, 3, 4, 5

This creates 5 jobs: train_1, train_2, train_3, train_4, train_5.

docs.title: Integer Range
docs.text:
parameters:
  i: "1:100"       # 1, 2, 3, ..., 100  (inclusive)
  i: "0:100:10"    # 0, 10, 20, ..., 100  (with step)

docs.title: List
docs.text:
parameters:
  split: "[train, test, val]"
  batch: "[32, 64, 128]"
  lr: "[0.001, 0.01, 0.1]"

docs.title: Template Substitution
docs.text:
Use {param_name} or {param_name:format} in names, commands, paths, and dependency lists:

- name: "job_{i:03d}"          # job_001, job_042, job_100
  command: "python run.py {i}"
  output_files: ["output_{i:03d}"]

docs.title: Format Specifiers
docs.text: Specifier Example Output {i} i=5 5 {i:03d} i=5 005 {i:05d} i=42 00042 {lr:.4f} lr=0.001 0.0010 {lr:.2e} lr=0.001 1.00e-03 {name} name=train train

docs.title: Cartesian Product (default)
docs.text:
All combinations of all parameters:

jobs:
  - name: "train_{lr:.4f}_bs{batch}"
    command: "python train.py --lr {lr} --batch {batch}"
    parameters:
      lr: "[0.001, 0.01]"
      batch: "[32, 64]"
    parameter_mode: cartesian   # default

Produces 4 jobs: (0.001,32), (0.001,64), (0.01,32), (0.01,64).

docs.title: Zip Mode
docs.text:
Pairs parameters positionally (like Python's zip):

jobs:
  - name: "process_{dataset}_{model}"
    command: "python run.py --data {dataset} --model {model}"
    parameters:
      dataset: "[train, val, test]"
      model: "[small, medium, large]"
    parameter_mode: zip

Produces 3 jobs: (train,small), (val,medium), (test,large).

docs.title: Parameterized Files
docs.text:
Files can also be parameterized to match parameterized jobs:

files:
  - name: "model_{lr:.4f}"
    path: "models/model_{lr:.4f}.pkl"
    parameters:
      lr: "[0.001, 0.01, 0.1]"

jobs:
  - name: "train_{lr:.4f}"
    command: "python train.py --lr {lr}"
    parameters:
      lr: "[0.001, 0.01, 0.1]"
    output_files: ["model_{lr:.4f}"]

docs.title: Regex Dependencies for Parameterized Jobs
docs.text:
Use depends_on_regexes to collect all parameterized jobs into a final step:

jobs:
  - name: train_{fold}
    command: python train.py --fold {fold}
    parameters:
      fold: "1:10"

  - name: aggregate
    command: python aggregate.py
    depends_on_regexes:
      - "train_.*"    # waits for all 10 train jobs

docs.title: Full Example: Hyperparameter Sweep
docs.text:
sweep.yaml
name: hyperparameter-sweep

files:
  - name: dataset
    path: data/train.csv
    st_mtime: 1710000000.0
  - name: checkpoint_{lr:.4f}_{bs}
    path: checkpoints/ckpt_{lr:.4f}_{bs}.pt
    parameters:
      lr: "[0.001, 0.01]"
      bs: "[32, 64]"

jobs:
  - name: train_{lr:.4f}_{bs}
    command: >
      python train.py
        --lr {lr}
        --batch {bs}
        --out checkpoints/ckpt_{lr:.4f}_{bs}.pt
    parameters:
      lr: "[0.001, 0.01]"
      bs: "[32, 64]"
    input_files: [dataset]
    output_files: [checkpoint_{lr:.4f}_{bs}]
    resource_requirements:
      num_cpus: 4
      memory: "8g"
      runtime: "PT1H"

  - name: select_best
    command: python select_best.py checkpoints/
    depends_on_regexes:
      - "train_.*"

Running this creates 4 training jobs plus 1 final aggregation job.

docs.title: Resource Management
docs.text: TorcPy tracks CPU, memory, and GPU resources on each worker node. Jobs only run when sufficient resources are available.

docs.title: Resource Requirements Spec
docs.text:
Define per-job resource requirements inline:

jobs:
  - name: heavy_job
    command: python compute.py
    resource_requirements:
      num_cpus: 8         # number of CPU cores
      num_gpus: 2         # number of GPU devices
      num_nodes: 1        # number of compute nodes
      memory: "32g"       # memory requirement
      runtime: "PT4H"     # maximum walltime (ISO 8601)

docs.title: Memory Format
docs.text:
Memory is specified as a string with a unit suffix:

Value Bytes "512k" 524,288 "1m" 1,048,576 "4g" 4,294,967,296 "1t" 1,099,511,627,776

Case-insensitive. The b suffix is optional ("4gb" = "4g").

docs.title: Runtime Format
docs.text:
Runtime uses ISO 8601 duration format:

Value Duration "PT30M" 30 minutes "PT2H" 2 hours "PT1H30M" 1 hour 30 minutes "P1D" 1 day "PT90S" 90 seconds

docs.title: Resource Auto-Detection
docs.text:
Workers automatically detect available resources on startup:

from torcpy.client.resource_tracker import ResourceTracker

tracker = ResourceTracker.detect_local()
print(f"CPUs: {tracker.total_cpus}")
print(f"Memory: {tracker.total_memory_bytes / 1e9:.1f} GB")
print(f"GPUs: {tracker.total_gpus}")

GPU detection uses CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES if set, otherwise queries nvidia-smi.

docs.title: Scheduling Behaviour
docs.text:
The job runner only claims a new job if it fits within available resources:

available_cpus  >= job.num_cpus
available_mem   >= job.memory_bytes
available_gpus  >= job.num_gpus

If a job doesn't fit, it is returned to ready status and will be claimed later.

docs.title: Claim Sort Order
docs.text:
Control how the server prioritises ready jobs when multiple workers compete:

Sort Method Behaviour priority (default) Higher priority value scheduled first gpus_runtime_memory Most GPU-hungry jobs first cpus_runtime_memory Most CPU-hungry jobs first

Set in the Python client:

from torcpy.models.enums import ClaimJobsSortMethod

jobs = await client.claim_next_jobs(
    workflow_id=1,
    count=5,
    sort=ClaimJobsSortMethod.GPUS_RUNTIME_MEMORY,
)

docs.title: Job Priority
docs.text:
Set priority on individual jobs to influence scheduling order (higher = sooner):

jobs:
  - name: critical_step
    command: python critical.py
    priority: 100     # runs before lower-priority jobs

  - name: background_step
    command: python bg.py
    priority: 0       # default

docs.title: Workflow Specification
docs.text: TorcPy workflows are defined in YAML or JSON files. The spec describes the workflow metadata, files, user data, jobs, schedulers, and failure handlers.

docs.title: File Formats
docs.text: Extension Format Notes .yaml, .yml YAML Recommended for readability .json JSON Strict JSON .json5 JSON5 JSON with comments (requires json5 package)

docs.title: Top-Level Structure
docs.text:
name: my-workflow          # required
user: alice                # optional, defaults to $USER
project: ml-project        # optional grouping label
metadata:                  # optional arbitrary JSON
  version: "1.0"

files:        []           # file artifacts
user_data:    []           # key-value data
jobs:         []           # computational tasks
schedulers:   []           # execution environments
failure_handlers: []       # retry/recovery rules

docs.title: files
docs.text:
Files represent input/output artifacts. They create implicit dependencies between jobs.

files:
  - name: input             # required, unique name
    path: data/input.csv    # filesystem path (optional)
    st_mtime: 1710000000.0  # set for pre-existing inputs; omit for outputs

docs.title: user_data
docs.text:
Named data items (JSON values) that can be passed between jobs.

user_data:
  - name: hyperparams
    data:
      learning_rate: 0.001
      batch_size: 64
    is_ephemeral: false     # true = deleted after workflow completes

docs.title: jobs
docs.text:
The core of the workflow — each job is a unit of computation.

jobs:
  - name: train             # required, unique name
    command: python train.py --lr 0.001

    # Dependencies
    depends_on: [preprocess]          # explicit job names
    depends_on_regexes: ["train_.*"]  # regex patterns

    # File relationships
    input_files: [features]
    output_files: [model]

    # Data relationships
    input_user_data: [hyperparams]
    output_user_data: [metrics]

    # Execution
    priority: 10                     # higher = scheduled first (default 0)
    cancel_on_blocking_job_failure: false
    supports_termination: false      # true = handle SIGTERM gracefully

    # Resources
    resource_requirements:
      num_cpus: 4
      num_gpus: 1
      num_nodes: 1
      memory: "8g"         # 1k, 512m, 2g, 1t
      runtime: "PT2H"      # ISO 8601 duration

    # Failure handling
    failure_handler: retry_on_oom

    # Parameterization
    parameters:
      fold: "1:5"
      lr: "[0.001, 0.01, 0.1]"
    parameter_mode: cartesian   # or "zip"

docs.title: schedulers
docs.text:
LocalSlurm 
schedulers:
  - type: local
    num_cpus: 8
    memory: "32g"

schedulers:
  - type: slurm
    account: myproject
    partition: gpu
    slurm_config:
      gres: gpu:1
      time: "02:00:00"

docs.title: failure_handlers
docs.text:
Define retry rules triggered by specific exit codes:

failure_handlers:
  - name: retry_on_oom
    rules:
      - exit_codes: [137, 139]       # OOM kill / segfault
        max_retries: 2
        recovery_command: echo "Retrying after OOM"
      - exit_code_ranges: [[1, 10]]  # any exit 1–10
        max_retries: 1
    default_max_retries: 0

docs.title: Complete Example
docs.text:
full_example.yaml
name: hyperparameter-sweep
user: alice
project: nlp-research

files:
  - name: corpus
    path: data/corpus.txt
    st_mtime: 1710000000.0
  - name: model_{lr:.4f}
    path: models/model_{lr:.4f}.pkl
    parameters:
      lr: "[0.001, 0.01, 0.1]"

user_data:
  - name: best_lr
  - name: training_config
    data: {epochs: 10, seed: 42}

failure_handlers:
  - name: retry_oom
    rules:
      - exit_codes: [137]
        max_retries: 2

jobs:
  - name: train_{lr:.4f}
    command: python train.py --lr {lr:.4f} --output models/model_{lr:.4f}.pkl
    parameters:
      lr: "[0.001, 0.01, 0.1]"
    input_files: [corpus]
    output_files: [model_{lr:.4f}]
    input_user_data: [training_config]
    resource_requirements:
      num_cpus: 4
      memory: "8g"
      runtime: "PT1H"
    failure_handler: retry_oom
    priority: 10

  - name: select_best
    command: python select_best.py
    depends_on_regexes: ["train_.*"]
    output_user_data: [best_lr]

  - name: final_report
    command: python report.py
    depends_on: [select_best]
    input_user_data: [best_lr]

docs.title: Design & Architecture
docs.text:
Internal design documentation for contributors and advanced users.

Document Description Architecture System overview and component interactions Database Schema Complete table schema and index rationale Server Design FastAPI server patterns and concurrency model Client Design Client library and job runner internals Job Runner Resource tracking and parallel execution Dependency Graph How deps are built, stored, and resolved

docs.title: System Overview
docs.text:
TorcPy follows a client-server architecture. A single server process manages persistent state in SQLite. Multiple worker processes communicate with the server over HTTP.

flowchart TB
    subgraph server_box ["Server (single process)"]
        FAST["FastAPI\n(uvicorn)"]
        BG["Background Unblock Task\n(asyncio.Task)"]
        DB[("SQLite\n(WAL mode)")]
        FAST <--> DB
        BG <--> DB
    end

    subgraph workers_box ["Workers (N processes)"]
        W1["Worker 1\nJobRunner"]
        W2["Worker 2\nJobRunner"]
    end

    CLI["CLI / Python Client\n(TorcClient)"]

    CLI -- "CRUD\nworkflows/jobs/files" --> FAST
    W1 -- "claim / complete" --> FAST
    W2 -- "claim / complete" --> FAST
    FAST -- "signals" --> BG

docs.title: Deferred Dependency Unblocking
docs.text:
Job completion is a hot path — many workers may complete jobs simultaneously. To keep complete_job fast:

  1. complete_job sets unblocking_processed = 0 and immediately returns.
  2. A background asyncio.Task runs every ~1 second, scanning for unprocessed completions.
  3. The background task unblocks dependents in batch.

This means there is a ~1 second delay between job completion and dependents becoming ready. For most workflows this is negligible; for very latency-sensitive pipelines, the interval is configurable.

docs.title: Write-Locked Job Claiming
docs.text:
Multiple workers may simultaneously request jobs. To prevent double-allocation:

BEGIN IMMEDIATE;
SELECT id FROM job WHERE workflow_id=? AND status=2 ORDER BY priority DESC LIMIT ?;
UPDATE job SET status=3 WHERE id IN (...);
COMMIT;

BEGIN IMMEDIATE acquires a write lock before any reads, ensuring exclusivity. Other workers block (up to the 5-second busy timeout) and then proceed with the remaining ready jobs.

docs.title: Cascade Deletes
docs.text: Every child table references workflow with ON DELETE CASCADE. Deleting a workflow removes all jobs, files, results, events, compute nodes, and schedulers atomically.

docs.title: No External Dependencies
docs.text: TorcPy has no runtime dependency on external services (no Redis, RabbitMQ, PostgreSQL). The server and database run in a single process. This makes deployment trivial and testing straightforward.

docs.title: Concurrency Model
docs.text:
• Server: Single uvicorn process, asyncio event loop, multiple coroutines.
  • Database: Single connection with WAL mode. Writes are serialized; reads are concurrent.
  • Workers: Separate OS processes, each with their own asyncio event loop.
  • Job execution: Each worker runs jobs as asyncio.subprocess tasks, with asyncio.gather for parallelism within a single worker.

docs.title: Module Structure
docs.text:
src/torcpy/client/
├── api_client.py          # TorcClient — async HTTP client
├── workflow_spec.py       # WorkflowSpec + create_workflow_from_spec()
├── parameter_expansion.py # Parameter ranges, lists, Cartesian product
├── job_runner.py          # JobRunner — local parallel execution engine
├── resource_tracker.py    # ResourceTracker — CPU/memory/GPU accounting
└── async_command.py       # run_command() — non-blocking subprocess

docs.title: TorcClient
docs.text:
TorcClient wraps every REST endpoint with an async method. It uses httpx.AsyncClient internally and supports both manual lifecycle management and use as an async context manager.

# Context manager (recommended)
async with TorcClient(base_url="http://localhost:8080/torcpy/v1") as client:
    workflow = await client.create_workflow(CreateWorkflow(name="my-pipeline"))

# Manual lifecycle
client = TorcClient()
await client.__aenter__()
# ... use client ...
await client.__aexit__(None, None, None)

docs.title: Base URL Resolution
docs.text:
The client resolves its base URL in priority order:

  1. base_url constructor argument
  2. TORCPY_API_URL environment variable
  3. Default: http://localhost:8080/torcpy/v1

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
httpx.HTTPStatusError is raised for 4xx/5xx responses (via response.raise_for_status()). Callers should handle:

  • 404 Not Found — resource does not exist
  • 422 Unprocessable Entity — validation error (response body has field-level details)
  • 409 Conflict — state conflict (e.g., canceling an already-completed workflow)

docs.title: WorkflowSpec
docs.text:
WorkflowSpec is a Pydantic model that represents the declarative format users write in YAML, JSON, or JSON5 files.

@classmethod
def from_file(cls, path: Path) -> "WorkflowSpec":
    # Dispatches on suffix: .yaml/.yml → PyYAML
    #                        .json5    → json5 library
    #                        .json     → stdlib json

docs.title: create_workflow_from_spec()
docs.text:
Translates a WorkflowSpec into live server resources in a fixed order:

WorkflowSpec
  │
  ├─► POST /workflows            → workflow_id
  ├─► POST /workflows/{id}/files (for each file in spec)
  ├─► POST /workflows/{id}/user_data
  ├─► POST /workflows/{id}/failure_handlers
  ├─► POST /workflows/{id}/schedulers
  └─► Job creation (two passes)
        Pass 1: create all jobs without depends_on → get job IDs
        Pass 2: for jobs with explicit depends_on:
                  resolve names → IDs, DELETE job, re-create with depends_on_job_ids

Why two passes? YAML specs use human-readable job names in depends_on. A job may depend on another job defined later in the file. Two passes ensure all job IDs exist before resolving cross-references.

docs.title: Parameter Expansion
docs.text:
Parameterized specs generate multiple job instances from a single JobSpec with a parameters field.

class ParameterValue:
    @staticmethod
    def parse(value: str | list) -> ParameterValue: ...
    def values(self) -> list[Any]: ...

docs.title: Supported Formats
docs.text: Format Example Result Integer range "1:5" [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] Range with step "0:100:10" [0, 10, 20, ..., 100] Float range "0.0:1.0:0.25" [0.0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0] List "[1, 5, 10]" [1, 5, 10] String list "['a', 'b']" ['a', 'b'] Literal 42 [42]

docs.title: Expansion Modes
docs.text:
• cartesian (default): Cartesian product of all parameter lists
  • zip: Element-wise pairing (all lists must have the same length)

docs.title: Template Substitution
docs.text:
substitute_template("{lr:.4f}_batch{bs:03d}", {"lr": 0.001, "bs": 32})
# → "0.0010_batch032"

Substitution uses Python's str.format_map() with format specifiers — any valid Python format spec works (d, f, s, e, g, 03d, .2f, etc.).

docs.title: JobRunner
docs.text:
JobRunner drives local parallel execution. It polls the server for ready jobs, enforces resource constraints, launches subprocesses, and reports results back to the server.

config = JobRunnerConfig(
    workflow_id=42,
    output_dir=Path("output"),
    stdio_mode=StdioMode.SEPARATE,
    poll_interval=2.0,
    max_concurrent_jobs=8,
)
runner = JobRunner(client, config, resource_tracker)
await runner.run()

docs.title: Execution Loop
docs.text:
while workflow not terminal:
    jobs = await client.claim_next_jobs(workflow_id, n=slots_available)
    for job in jobs:
        task = asyncio.create_task(run_job(job))
        active_tasks.add(task)
    done, pending = await asyncio.wait(active_tasks, timeout=poll_interval)
    for task in done:
        result = task.result()
        await client.complete_job(workflow_id, job_id, result)
        resource_tracker.release(job.resource_requirements)

docs.title: Resource Accounting
docs.text: Before claiming jobs, the runner checks resource_tracker.can_fit() to determine how many concurrent slots are available. Resources are allocated on claim and released on completion.

docs.title: Shutdown
docs.text:
JobRunner installs SIGINT/SIGTERM handlers. On signal:

  1. Stop claiming new jobs
  2. Wait for all active tasks to complete
  3. Return gracefully

docs.title: ResourceTracker
docs.text:
ResourceTracker maintains counts of available CPUs, memory (bytes), and GPUs:

tracker = ResourceTracker.detect_local()  # auto-detects system resources
tracker = ResourceTracker(cpus=16, memory_bytes=64 * 1024**3, gpus=2)

if tracker.can_fit(job.resource_requirements):
    tracker.allocate(job.resource_requirements)
    # ... run job ...
    tracker.release(job.resource_requirements)

detect_local() uses os.cpu_count(), psutil.virtual_memory(), and CUDA device enumeration (falls back to 0 if unavailable).

docs.title: AsyncCommand
docs.text:
run_command() launches a shell subprocess asynchronously:

result = await run_command(
    command="python train.py --lr 0.001",
    stdout_path=Path("output/job_42.stdout"),
    stderr_path=Path("output/job_42.stderr"),
    walltime_seconds=3600.0,
)

Walltime enforcement uses asyncio.wait_for() wrapping process.wait(). On timeout, the process group is killed with SIGKILL and the job is marked terminated.

docs.title: Stdio Modes
docs.text: StdioMode Files Written separate job_N.stdout + job_N.stderr combined job_N.log (merged) no_stdout job_N.stderr only no_stderr job_N.stdout only none No files — output discarded

docs.title: Database Schema
docs.text: TorcPy uses a single SQLite file. All tables are created at server startup via Database.init_schema() using the SCHEMA_SQL constant in server/database.py.

docs.title: Critical Indexes
docs.text: Index Purpose idx_job_workflow_status Fast job listing by workflow + status idx_job_workflow_status_priority Claim ordering (priority DESC) idx_job_unblocking_pending Background task scan (partial index) idx_job_depends_on_depends_on_job_id Unblocking: find dependents of completed job

docs.title: Partial Index for Background Task
docs.text:
CREATE INDEX idx_job_unblocking_pending
ON job(workflow_id, unblocking_processed)
WHERE status IN (5,6,7,8) AND unblocking_processed=0;

This index is tiny (only completed/failed jobs with pending unblock) and makes the background task scan extremely fast even with millions of jobs.

docs.title: Migration Strategy
docs.text:
TorcPy uses raw CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS for initial schema creation. For subsequent migrations, use Alembic (alembic/).

SQLite CASCADE Safety

Never use the rename-recreate pattern (CREATE new table, INSERT...SELECT, DROP old) for tables that have ON DELETE CASCADE foreign keys. Inside a transaction, PRAGMA foreign_keys=OFF is silently ignored — DROP TABLE will cascade-delete child rows. Use ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN instead.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
TorcPy supports two kinds of job dependencies:

  • Explicit: declared directly in the workflow spec via depends_on
  • Implicit: inferred automatically from shared files and user data

Both kinds are stored in the same job_depends_on table. The server treats them identically at runtime — the distinction only matters during the initialize phase when the graph is built.

docs.title: Explicit Dependencies
docs.text:
Declared in the spec using job names:

jobs:
  - name: preprocess
    command: python preprocess.py

  - name: train
    command: python train.py
    depends_on:
      - preprocess

  - name: evaluate
    command: python evaluate.py
    depends_on:
      - train

During create_workflow_from_spec(), names are resolved to job IDs in a two-pass process:

  1. Pass 1: All jobs are created without depends_on to get their IDs
  2. Pass 2: Jobs with depends_on are deleted and re-created with depends_on_job_ids
job_depends_on
  upstream_job_id  │  downstream_job_id
  ─────────────────┼───────────────────
        1          │        2          (preprocess → train)
        2          │        3          (train → evaluate)

docs.title: Implicit File Dependencies
docs.text:
When two jobs share a file — one as output, one as input — the server automatically inserts a dependency between them during initialize.

files:
  - name: preprocessed_data
    path: /data/preprocessed.parquet

jobs:
  - name: preprocess
    command: python preprocess.py
    output_files: [preprocessed_data]

  - name: train
    command: python train.py
    input_files: [preprocessed_data]

The server queries:

-- Find all (producer, consumer) pairs via shared files
SELECT DISTINCT
    pj.id AS upstream_job_id,
    cj.id AS downstream_job_id
FROM job_file jf_out
JOIN job_file jf_in ON jf_out.file_id = jf_in.file_id
JOIN job pj ON jf_out.job_id = pj.id
JOIN job cj ON jf_in.job_id = cj.id
WHERE jf_out.is_output = 1
  AND jf_in.is_output = 0
  AND pj.workflow_id = ?
  AND cj.workflow_id = ?
  AND pj.id != cj.id

These are inserted into job_depends_on as implicit rows (same table, same effect).

docs.title: User Data Dependencies
docs.text:
The same implicit dependency logic applies to user_data:

user_data:
  - name: model_weights
    key: weights_v2

jobs:
  - name: train
    command: python train.py
    output_user_data: [model_weights]

  - name: serve
    command: python serve.py
    input_user_data: [model_weights]

docs.title: Initialization Algorithm
docs.text:
POST /workflows/{id}/initialize runs the following algorithm:

1. Insert all implicit file dependencies
2. Insert all implicit user_data dependencies
3. Skip duplicates (explicit deps may overlap with implicit ones)

4. Compute ready set:
   SELECT id FROM job j
   WHERE j.workflow_id = ?
     AND j.status = 0  -- uninitialized
     AND NOT EXISTS (
       SELECT 1 FROM job_depends_on d
       JOIN job upstream ON d.upstream_job_id = upstream.id
       WHERE d.downstream_job_id = j.id
         AND upstream.status != 5  -- not completed
     )

5. Mark jobs with no pending upstream dependencies → READY (status=2)
6. Mark remaining uninitialized jobs → BLOCKED (status=1)

docs.title: Unblocking Algorithm
docs.text:
When a job completes (status → 5, 6, 7, or 8), the background unblock task processes it:

For each completed job C:
  If C.status == COMPLETED:
    For each downstream job D that depends on C:
      If all of D's upstream dependencies are completed:
        D.status → READY
      Else:
        D remains BLOCKED

  If C.status IN (FAILED, CANCELED, TERMINATED):
    For each downstream job D:
      D.status → CANCELED  (cascade cancellation)
      Recursively cancel D's dependents

  C.unblocking_processed = 1

This runs in the BackgroundUnblockTask rather than inline in the HTTP handler, batching all pending unblocks together for efficiency.

docs.title: Cycle Detection
docs.text:
Circular dependencies cause all jobs in the cycle to remain BLOCKED indefinitely — no job can become READY because each is waiting for another in the cycle.

TorcPy does not currently detect cycles at creation time. If you suspect a cycle:

# Find jobs that are blocked but have all upstream jobs completed
sqlite3 torcpy.db "
SELECT j.id, j.name
FROM job j
WHERE j.workflow_id = 1
  AND j.status = 1  -- blocked
  AND NOT EXISTS (
    SELECT 1 FROM job_depends_on d
    JOIN job u ON d.upstream_job_id = u.id
    WHERE d.downstream_job_id = j.id
      AND u.status != 5
  );
"

Jobs returned by this query are blocked but have all dependencies satisfied — a sign of a data inconsistency or a bug in the unblocking logic.

docs.title: Diamond Dependencies
docs.text:
Diamond patterns are handled correctly:

    A
   / \
  B   C
   \ /
    D

D depends on both B and C. The unblock task checks all upstream dependencies before marking D as READY. D becomes ready only after both B and C complete.

-- D is ready when:
SELECT COUNT(*) = 0
FROM job_depends_on dep
JOIN job upstream ON dep.upstream_job_id = upstream.id
WHERE dep.downstream_job_id = D.id
  AND upstream.status != 5;

docs.title: Cascade Cancellation
docs.text:
When a job fails, all downstream dependents are canceled recursively:

async def _cancel_downstream(db, job_id, workflow_id):
    # Find direct dependents
    rows = await db.fetchall(
        "SELECT downstream_job_id FROM job_depends_on WHERE upstream_job_id = ?",
        (job_id,),
    )
    for row in rows:
        downstream_id = row["downstream_job_id"]
        await db.execute(
            "UPDATE job SET status = 7 WHERE id = ? AND status IN (1, 2)",
            (downstream_id,),
        )
        # Recurse
        await _cancel_downstream(db, downstream_id, workflow_id)

Only BLOCKED (1) and READY (2) jobs are canceled — jobs already RUNNING (4) or PENDING (3) are not canceled immediately (they will complete or fail on their own).

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The JobRunner is the local execution engine for TorcPy workflows. It sits between the server (which owns job state) and the operating system (where jobs actually execute as subprocesses).

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     JobRunner                       │
│                                                     │
│  ┌──────────────┐    ┌────────────┐                 │
│  │ claim_next   │───►│ can_fit?   │                 │
│  │ jobs (poll)  │    │ (resources)│                 │
│  └──────────────┘    └─────┬──────┘                 │
│                            │ yes                    │
│                     ┌──────▼──────┐                 │
│                     │  allocate   │                 │
│                     │  resources  │                 │
│                     └──────┬──────┘                 │
│                            │                        │
│                     ┌──────▼──────┐                 │
│                     │ asyncio     │                 │
│                     │ .create_    │                 │
│                     │  task()     │                 │
│                     └──────┬──────┘                 │
│                            │ on completion          │
│                     ┌──────▼──────┐                 │
│                     │ complete_   │                 │
│                     │ job (API)   │                 │
│                     └──────┬──────┘                 │
│                            │                        │
│                     ┌──────▼──────┐                 │
│                     │  release    │                 │
│                     │  resources  │                 │
│                     └─────────────┘                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
@dataclass
class JobRunnerConfig:
    workflow_id: int
    output_dir: Path = Path("output")
    stdio_mode: StdioMode = StdioMode.SEPARATE
    poll_interval: float = 2.0          # seconds between server polls
    max_concurrent_jobs: int | None = None  # None = unlimited (resource-limited only)
    claim_batch_size: int = 10          # jobs requested per claim call

docs.title: Execution Loop Detail
docs.text:
async def run(self) -> None:
    await self._setup_output_dir()
    self._install_signal_handlers()

    while not self._shutdown:
        # 1. Calculate how many more jobs we can accept
        slots = self._available_slots()
        if slots > 0:
            jobs = await self.client.claim_next_jobs(
                self.config.workflow_id,
                n=min(slots, self.config.claim_batch_size),
            )
            for job in jobs:
                self._launch(job)

        # 2. Check for workflow terminal state
        if await self._workflow_is_done():
            break

        # 3. Wait for tasks to complete or poll interval to elapse
        if self._active_tasks:
            done, _ = await asyncio.wait(
                self._active_tasks,
                timeout=self.config.poll_interval,
                return_when=asyncio.FIRST_COMPLETED,
            )
            await self._handle_completed(done)
        else:
            await asyncio.sleep(self.config.poll_interval)

    # 4. Drain remaining tasks
    if self._active_tasks:
        done, _ = await asyncio.wait(self._active_tasks)
        await self._handle_completed(done)

docs.title: Job Lifecycle (Single Job)
docs.text:
claim_next_jobs → job enters PENDING state on server
       │
       ▼
asyncio.create_task(_run_single_job(job))
       │
       ▼
run_command(job.command, stdout_path, stderr_path, walltime_seconds)
       │
       ├── success → exit_code=0
       │       └── complete_job(status=COMPLETED, result=...)
       │
       ├── nonzero exit → exit_code≠0
       │       └── complete_job(status=FAILED, result=...)
       │
       └── walltime exceeded → asyncio.TimeoutError
               └── complete_job(status=TERMINATED, result=...)

docs.title: Resource Slot Calculation
docs.text:
def _available_slots(self) -> int:
    resource_limited = self.resource_tracker.free_slots()
    if self.config.max_concurrent_jobs is not None:
        concurrency_limited = self.config.max_concurrent_jobs - len(self._active_tasks)
        return min(resource_limited, concurrency_limited)
    return resource_limited

ResourceTracker.free_slots() returns the number of additional jobs that could be accommodated given current free CPUs, memory, and GPUs. A job requiring 4 CPUs on a 16-CPU machine with 3 jobs already running (using 12 CPUs) yields 1 free slot.

docs.title: Result Reporting
docs.text:
When a job finishes, the runner sends a CompleteJob payload:

@dataclass
class JobResult:
    status: JobStatus          # COMPLETED / FAILED / TERMINATED
    exit_code: int | None
    stdout_path: str | None
    stderr_path: str | None
    start_time: float          # Unix timestamp
    end_time: float
    wall_time_seconds: float
    cpu_time_seconds: float | None
    max_memory_bytes: int | None

Resource metrics (CPU time, peak memory) are collected by reading /proc/{pid}/stat on Linux or via psutil cross-platform.

docs.title: Signal Handling
docs.text:
def _install_signal_handlers(self) -> None:
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    for sig in (signal.SIGINT, signal.SIGTERM):
        loop.add_signal_handler(sig, self._handle_shutdown_signal)

def _handle_shutdown_signal(self) -> None:
    self._shutdown = True
    # Active tasks are allowed to finish naturally
    # No new jobs will be claimed

On Ctrl-C, the runner stops claiming jobs but waits for all running jobs to finish before exiting. This prevents jobs from being left in PENDING state without a runner to complete them.

docs.title: Output Directory Layout
docs.text:
output/
  job_1.stdout      # stdio_mode=separate (default)
  job_1.stderr
  job_2.stdout
  job_2.stderr
  job_3.log         # stdio_mode=combined
  job_4.stderr      # stdio_mode=no_stdout

The output directory is created on runner.run() if it does not exist.

docs.title: Walltime Enforcement
docs.text:
async def _run_with_walltime(
    self,
    command: str,
    walltime: float | None,
    **kwargs,
) -> int:
    if walltime is None:
        return await run_command(command, **kwargs)
    try:
        return await asyncio.wait_for(run_command(command, **kwargs), timeout=walltime)
    except asyncio.TimeoutError:
        # Process group kill happens inside run_command's finally block
        raise

The walltime comes from job.resource_requirements.runtime_limit_seconds. Jobs without a runtime limit run until they exit naturally.

src/torcpy/server/
├── app.py          # FastAPI app factory + lifespan
├── database.py     # SQLite connection, schema, helpers
├── background.py   # BackgroundUnblockTask

└── api/
    ├── workflows.py

docs.title: App Factory
docs.text:
create_app(db_path) builds the FastAPI application with lifespan:

@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app):
    db = Database(db_path)
    await db.connect()
    await db.init_schema()
    app.state.db = db

    bg_task = BackgroundUnblockTask(db, interval=1.0)
    bg_task.start()
    app.state.bg_unblock = bg_task
    yield

    await bg_task.stop()
    await db.close()

docs.title: Database Layer
docs.text:
Database wraps a single aiosqlite.Connection:

# Regular transaction
async with db.transaction():
    await db.execute("INSERT ...")

# Write-locked transaction (job claiming)
async with db.write_transaction():
    rows = await db.fetchall("SELECT ...")
    await db.execute("UPDATE ...")

db.fetchone(), db.fetchall(), db.insert(), db.executemany() are convenience wrappers over the raw aiosqlite connection.

docs.title: Dependency Injection
docs.text:
Every route handler receives the database via FastAPI's dependency injection:

from torcpy.server.deps import get_db

@router.get("/{id}")
async def get_workflow(id: int, db: Database = Depends(get_db)):
    row = await db.fetchone("SELECT * FROM workflow WHERE id = ?", (id,))
    ...

get_db reads request.app.state.db, which was set during lifespan.

docs.title: Background Unblock Task
docs.text:
BackgroundUnblockTask runs as an asyncio.Task alongside the server:

class BackgroundUnblockTask:
    async def _run(self):
        while self._running:
            await asyncio.wait_for(self._event.wait(), timeout=self.interval)
            self._event.clear()
            await self._process_pending_unblocks()

    async def _process_pending_unblocks(self):
        # Find jobs with status IN (5,6,7,8) AND unblocking_processed=0
        # For each: unblock dependents or cancel downstream
        # Set unblocking_processed=1

The task is woken via bg_task.signal() from complete_job, ensuring it runs promptly after each completion rather than waiting for the full interval.

docs.title: JSON Serialization
docs.text:
Columns that store structured data (metadata, rules, slurm_config, etc.) are stored as JSON strings in SQLite and deserialized on read:

# Write
json.dumps(body.metadata) if body.metadata else None

# Read
json.loads(row["metadata"]) if row["metadata"] else None

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
Get TorcPy running in minutes.

Guide Description Installation Install TorcPy with uv or pip Quick Start (Local) Run your first workflow on this machine Quick Start (Server + Workers) Run workflows with a separate server and worker processes

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: TorcPy requires Python 3.11+.

docs.title: Using uv (Recommended)
docs.text:
uv is the recommended tool for installing TorcPy. It is significantly faster than pip and handles virtual environments automatically.

# Install uv if you don't have it
curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh

# Create a project and add TorcPy
uv add torcpy

# Or install globally into a virtual environment
uv pip install torcpy

docs.title: Using pip
docs.text: pip install torcpy

docs.title: Development Installation
docs.text:
To install from source with development dependencies:

git clone https://github.com/your-org/torcpy
cd torcpy
uv pip install -e ".[dev]"

docs.title: Verify Installation
docs.text:
torcpy --version
torcpy --help

docs.title: Optional Dependencies
docs.text: Feature Package Notes JSON5 spec files json5 Included by default YAML spec files pyyaml Included by default

docs.title: System Requirements
docs.text:
Component Requirement Python 3.11 or later SQLite 3.35+ (bundled with Python) OS Linux, macOS, Windows

No external services required

TorcPy uses SQLite — no PostgreSQL, Redis, or message broker required. The server and database run as a single process.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text: • Quick Start (Local) — Run your first workflow

docs.title: Quick Start (Local)
docs.text: This guide walks you through running your first TorcPy workflow on your local machine. Everything — server, database, and job execution — runs on the same host.

docs.title: 1. Start the Server
docs.text:
torcpy server run --db my_workflow.db

The server starts on http://localhost:8080 by default. Keep this terminal open.

docs.title: 2. Write a Workflow Spec
docs.text:
Save this as hello.yaml:

hello.yaml
name: hello-world

jobs:
  - name: greet
    command: echo "Hello from TorcPy!"

  - name: farewell
    command: echo "Goodbye from TorcPy!"
    depends_on: [greet]

docs.title: 3. Run the Workflow
docs.text:
In a second terminal:

torcpy run hello.yaml

Output:

Created workflow 1
Initialized: 1 ready, 1 blocked
Running job 1: greet
Running job 2: farewell

Workflow 1 finished:
  Completed: 2
  Failed:    0

docs.title: 4. Check Status
docs.text:
torcpy workflows status 1

Workflow 1
  Run ID: 0
  Total jobs: 2
  Job status:
    completed: 2

torcpy jobs list 1

 Jobs (workflow 1)
┏━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓
┃ ID ┃ Name     ┃ Status    ┃ Priority ┃ Command                       ┃
┡━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┩
│ 1  │ greet    │ completed │ 0        │ echo "Hello from TorcPy!"     │
│ 2  │ farewell │ completed │ 0        │ echo "Goodbye from TorcPy!"   │
└────┴──────────┴───────────┴──────────┴───────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: 5. Check Execution Results
docs.text: torcpy reports results 1

docs.title: Example: File-Dependency Pipeline
docs.text:
TorcPy can infer dependencies from shared files — no explicit depends_on needed:

pipeline.yaml
name: file-pipeline

files:
  - name: raw
    path: data/input.csv
    st_mtime: 1710000000.0   # (1) marks this as a pre-existing input file
  - name: processed
    path: data/processed.csv
  - name: report
    path: output/report.txt

jobs:
  - name: preprocess
    command: python preprocess.py
    input_files: [raw]
    output_files: [processed]

  - name: analyze
    command: python analyze.py
    input_files: [processed]   # (2) TorcPy infers: analyze depends on preprocess
    output_files: [report]

  - name: notify
    command: echo "Pipeline complete!"
    depends_on: [analyze]
  1. Setting st_mtime marks a file as pre-existing (not generated by the workflow). This distinguishes inputs from outputs.
  2. Because analyze reads processed, which is written by preprocess, TorcPy automatically inserts a dependency.
torcpy run pipeline.yaml

docs.title: Quick Start (Server + Workers)
docs.text: This guide shows how to run TorcPy with a dedicated server process and one or more separate worker processes. This is the typical production setup: the server manages state while workers claim and execute jobs.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    Server["TorcPy Server\n(FastAPI + SQLite)"]
    W1["Worker 1\n(torcpy run)"]
    W2["Worker 2\n(torcpy run)"]
    CLI["CLI\n(torcpy workflows ...)"]

    CLI -- "create / status" --> Server
    W1 -- "claim / complete jobs" --> Server
    W2 -- "claim / complete jobs" --> Server

docs.title: Step 1: Start the Server
docs.text:
On the server host:

torcpy server run --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8080 --db workflows.db

docs.title: Step 2: Create a Workflow
docs.text:
From any machine that can reach the server:

export TORCPY_API_URL=http://server-host:8080/torcpy/v1

torcpy workflows create pipeline.yaml
# Created workflow 3

docs.title: Step 3: Start Workers
docs.text:
On each worker machine, run the job runner pointing at the workflow:

export TORCPY_API_URL=http://server-host:8080/torcpy/v1

torcpy workflows run 3 --output-dir /scratch/job_logs

Workers automatically:

  1. Initialize the workflow (build the dependency graph)
  2. Poll the server for ready jobs
  3. Claim jobs using a write-locked transaction (no double-allocation)
  4. Execute jobs as subprocesses
  5. Report results back to the server
  6. Exit when all jobs are finished

docs.title: Step 4: Monitor Progress
docs.text:
torcpy workflows status 3
torcpy jobs list 3
torcpy reports summary 3

docs.title: Multiple Workers
docs.text:
You can run multiple workers against the same workflow simultaneously. TorcPy uses BEGIN IMMEDIATE transactions to ensure each job is claimed by exactly one worker:

# Terminal 1
torcpy workflows run 3 --output-dir /scratch/worker1

# Terminal 2
torcpy workflows run 3 --output-dir /scratch/worker2

Initialize only once

If multiple workers start simultaneously, only one should call initialize. Using torcpy workflows run handles this safely — it initializes if needed, then polls for work. Multiple simultaneous initialize calls are idempotent.

docs.title: Environment Variable
docs.text:
Set TORCPY_API_URL to avoid passing --url on every command:

export TORCPY_API_URL=http://server-host:8080/torcpy/v1

Or pass it inline:

torcpy --url http://server-host:8080/torcpy/v1 workflows list

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• CLI Cheat Sheet
  • Concepts: Architecture
  • How-To: Track Workflow Status

docs.title: How-To Guides
docs.text:
Targeted solutions to specific tasks. Each guide answers a single "how do I...?" question.

Guide Description Track Workflow Status Monitor job progress in real time Cancel a Workflow Stop a running workflow Debug a Failed Job Find out why a job failed Re-run Failed Jobs Retry failures without re-running everything Use the Python Library Use TorcPy programmatically in Python Parameterize Jobs with Files Match file names to parameterized jobs Set Resource Requirements Limit CPU, memory, and GPU per job

docs.title: Cancel via CLI
docs.text:
torcpy workflows cancel <workflow_id>

This immediately:

  1. Sets workflow_status.is_canceled = true
  2. Transitions all uninitialized, blocked, ready, and pending jobs to canceled

Running jobs are not interrupted

Jobs that are already running continue until they finish. Workers detect the cancellation flag on their next poll and stop claiming new jobs.

docs.title: Cancel via Python
docs.text:
from torcpy.client import TorcClient

async with TorcClient() as client:
    await client.cancel_workflow(workflow_id=42)

docs.title: Check Status After Cancellation
docs.text:
torcpy workflows status 42

Workflow 42
  Canceled: True
  Total jobs: 50
  Job status:
    completed: 23
    canceled: 27

docs.title: Resume After Cancellation
docs.text:
You cannot directly "un-cancel" a workflow, but you can reset and re-run it:

torcpy workflows reset 42
torcpy workflows run 42

reset sets all jobs back to uninitialized and increments the run_id. The next run call will re-initialize and re-execute only jobs that need to run.

Warning

reset re-runs all jobs, including ones that previously completed. See Re-run Failed Jobs for a more targeted approach.

docs.title: 1. Find Failed Jobs
docs.text: torcpy jobs list <workflow_id> --status 6

docs.title: 2. Inspect a Specific Job
docs.text:
torcpy jobs get <workflow_id> <job_id>

Job 47: process_data_batch_023
  Status: failed
  Command: python process.py --batch 23
  Priority: 0
  Depends on: [12]

docs.title: 3. Check Execution Results
docs.text:
torcpy reports results <workflow_id> --format json | python -m json.tool

Look for:

  • return_code — non-zero indicates the command failed
  • exec_time_minutes — was it killed by walltime?
  • status — "failed" vs "completed"

docs.title: 4. Read Job Logs
docs.text:
By default, TorcPy saves stdout and stderr for each job in the output directory:

output/
  job_47.stdout
  job_47.stderr

cat output/job_47.stderr

If you ran with --output-dir /path/to/logs:

cat /path/to/logs/job_47.stderr

docs.title: 5. Re-run the Job Manually
docs.text:
To reproduce the failure:

# Get the exact command
torcpy jobs get <workflow_id> <job_id> --format json | python -c "import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['command'])"

# Run it directly
python process.py --batch 23

docs.title: 6. Reset and Retry the Job
docs.text:
After fixing the issue:

# Reset just this one job back to uninitialized
torcpy jobs update <workflow_id> <job_id> --status 0

# Re-initialize and run
torcpy workflows initialize <workflow_id>
torcpy workflows run <workflow_id>

Or reset all failed jobs at once via the Python client:

from torcpy.client import TorcClient
from torcpy.models.enums import JobStatus
from torcpy.models.job import JobUpdate

async with TorcClient() as client:
    result = await client.list_jobs(workflow_id, status=JobStatus.FAILED)
    for job in result["items"]:
        await client.update_job(
            workflow_id,
            job["id"],
            JobUpdate(status=JobStatus.UNINITIALIZED),
        )
    await client.initialize_workflow(workflow_id)

docs.title: Common Failure Causes
docs.text: Symptom Likely Cause return_code: 1 Script error — check stderr return_code: 137 OOM kill (memory limit exceeded) return_code: -1 Walltime exceeded Job stuck in pending Worker crashed before completing the job pending_failed status Failure handler couldn't match the exit code

docs.text:

docs.title: How-To: Parameterize Jobs with Files
docs.text: When each parameterized job reads/writes a unique file, use template substitution in both the job and file specs to create matching pairs.

docs.title: Pattern
docs.text:
files:
  - name: output_{i:03d}
    path: results/output_{i:03d}.csv
    parameters:
      i: "1:10"

jobs:
  - name: process_{i:03d}
    command: python process.py --id {i} --output results/output_{i:03d}.csv
    parameters:
      i: "1:10"
    output_files: [output_{i:03d}]   # template matched to file name

TorcPy expands both specs with the same parameters, producing pairs: - process_001 → writes output_001 - process_002 → writes output_002 - ...

docs.title: Full Example: Data Shards
docs.text:
shards.yaml
name: shard-processing

files:
  - name: shard_{i:03d}_input
    path: data/shards/shard_{i:03d}.parquet
    st_mtime: 1710000000.0   # pre-existing inputs
    parameters:
      i: "1:50"

  - name: shard_{i:03d}_output
    path: results/shards/shard_{i:03d}_processed.parquet
    parameters:
      i: "1:50"

  - name: final_dataset
    path: results/final.parquet

jobs:
  - name: process_shard_{i:03d}
    command: >
      python process_shard.py
        --input data/shards/shard_{i:03d}.parquet
        --output results/shards/shard_{i:03d}_processed.parquet
    parameters:
      i: "1:50"
    input_files: [shard_{i:03d}_input]
    output_files: [shard_{i:03d}_output]
    resource_requirements:
      num_cpus: 2
      memory: "4g"
      runtime: "PT30M"

  - name: merge
    command: python merge_shards.py --input-dir results/shards/ --output results/final.parquet
    depends_on_regexes:
      - "process_shard_.*"
    output_files: [final_dataset]

docs.title: Key Points
docs.text:
• File name and job output_files/input_files references must use identical templates with the same parameter names and format specifiers.
  • The st_mtime field on input shards marks them as pre-existing (no job produces them).
  • Implicit dependencies flow from file relationships — merge has no explicit depends_on, but TorcPy inserts dependencies because it reads output files of the processing jobs.

docs.title: How-To: Re-run Failed Jobs
docs.text: Re-run only the failed (or specific) jobs without resetting the entire workflow.

docs.title: Reset All Failed Jobs
docs.text:
from torcpy.client import TorcClient
from torcpy.models.enums import JobStatus
from torcpy.models.job import JobUpdate
import asyncio

async def rerun_failed(workflow_id: int):
    async with TorcClient() as client:
        # Get all failed jobs
        result = await client.list_jobs(workflow_id, status=int(JobStatus.FAILED))
        failed_jobs = result["items"]
        print(f"Resetting {len(failed_jobs)} failed jobs...")

        for job in failed_jobs:
            await client.update_job(
                workflow_id,
                job["id"],
                JobUpdate(status=JobStatus.UNINITIALIZED),
            )

        # Re-initialize (rebuilds dep graph, marks newly-ready jobs)
        await client.initialize_workflow(workflow_id)
        print("Ready to run")

asyncio.run(rerun_failed(42))

Then run the worker again:

torcpy workflows run 42

docs.title: Reset a Single Job
docs.text:
# Via CLI — set status back to uninitialized (0)
torcpy jobs update <workflow_id> <job_id> --status 0
torcpy workflows initialize <workflow_id>
torcpy workflows run <workflow_id>

docs.title: Reset Terminated Jobs
docs.text:
Jobs that were terminated (walltime exceeded) can be reset the same way:

result = await client.list_jobs(workflow_id, status=int(JobStatus.TERMINATED))
for job in result["items"]:
    await client.update_job(workflow_id, job["id"], JobUpdate(status=JobStatus.UNINITIALIZED))

docs.title: Reset the Whole Workflow
docs.text:
To start completely from scratch:

torcpy workflows reset <workflow_id>
torcpy workflows run <workflow_id>

reset increments the run_id and returns all jobs to uninitialized.

docs.title: Preserve Completed Jobs
docs.text:
If you want to skip already-completed jobs when re-running, the current approach is to leave them in completed status. During initialize, only uninitialized jobs are processed — completed jobs are left as-is and won't be re-claimed.

Tip

Only reset (status=0) the jobs you actually want to re-run. Completed jobs will remain completed and their dependents will already be resolved correctly.

docs.title: How-To: Set Resource Requirements
docs.text: Resource requirements tell TorcPy how much CPU, memory, and GPU a job needs. Workers only claim jobs that fit within available resources.

docs.title: In the Workflow Spec
docs.text:
jobs:
  - name: gpu_train
    command: python train.py
    resource_requirements:
      num_cpus: 8       # number of CPU cores
      num_gpus: 1       # number of GPU devices
      num_nodes: 1      # number of compute nodes
      memory: "32g"     # memory in k/m/g/t
      runtime: "PT4H"   # max walltime (ISO 8601)

docs.title: Memory Formats
docs.text: Value Amount "512k" 512 KiB "1m" 1 MiB "8g" 8 GiB "2t" 2 TiB

docs.title: Runtime Formats (ISO 8601)
docs.text: Value Duration "PT30M" 30 minutes "PT2H" 2 hours "PT1H30M" 1 hour 30 minutes "P1D" 1 day

docs.title: Omitting Resource Requirements
docs.text: Jobs with no resource_requirements are always considered to fit and will be claimed immediately (no resource checking). This is fine for lightweight jobs like shell commands.

docs.title: Via the Python Client
docs.text:
from torcpy.client import TorcClient
from torcpy.models import ResourceRequirementsCreate

async with TorcClient() as client:
    rr = await client.create_resource_requirements(
        workflow_id=1,
        body=ResourceRequirementsCreate(
            workflow_id=1,
            num_cpus=4,
            memory="16g",
            runtime="PT2H",
        ),
    )
    # Use rr.id in JobCreate(resource_requirements_id=rr.id, ...)

docs.title: Checking What Workers Detect
docs.text:
from torcpy.client.resource_tracker import ResourceTracker

tracker = ResourceTracker.detect_local()
print(f"CPUs available: {tracker.total_cpus}")
print(f"Memory available: {tracker.total_memory_bytes / 1e9:.1f} GB")
print(f"GPUs available: {tracker.total_gpus}")

docs.title: Get a Status Summary
docs.text:
torcpy workflows status <workflow_id>

Output:

Workflow 42
  Run ID: 0
  Canceled: False
  Total jobs: 101
  Job status:
    completed: 87
    running: 6
    pending: 2
    ready: 5
    blocked: 1

docs.title: List All Jobs
docs.text:
torcpy jobs list <workflow_id>

Filter by status (use the integer code):

# Show only failed jobs (status=6)
torcpy jobs list <workflow_id> --status 6

# Show only running jobs (status=4)
torcpy jobs list <workflow_id> --status 4

docs.title: Get JSON Output
docs.text:
All commands support --format json:

torcpy workflows status 42 --format json
torcpy jobs list 42 --format json | python -m json.tool

docs.title: Continuous Monitoring (shell loop)
docs.text:
while true; do
    clear
    torcpy workflows status 42
    sleep 5
done

docs.title: Python Client
docs.text:
import asyncio
from torcpy.client import TorcClient

async def monitor(workflow_id: int):
    async with TorcClient() as client:
        while True:
            status = await client.workflow_status(workflow_id)
            counts = status["job_status_counts"]
            total = status["total_jobs"]
            done = counts.get("completed", 0) + counts.get("failed", 0)

            print(f"Progress: {done}/{total}")
            print(f"  running={counts.get('running',0)}")
            print(f"  failed={counts.get('failed',0)}")

            if done == total:
                break

            await asyncio.sleep(5)

asyncio.run(monitor(42))

docs.title: Job Status Codes
docs.text:
Code Name 0 uninitialized 1 blocked 2 ready 3 pending 4 running 5 completed 6 failed 7 canceled 8 terminated 9 disabled 10 pending_failed

See Job States for the full reference.

docs.title: How-To: Use the Python Library
docs.text: TorcPy ships a fully-typed async Python client. Use it to build custom orchestration scripts, integrate with notebooks, or embed TorcPy into larger applications.

docs.title: Basic Setup
docs.text:
from torcpy.client import TorcClient

# Use as an async context manager (recommended)
async with TorcClient("http://localhost:8080/torcpy/v1") as client:
    wf = await client.get_workflow(1)
    print(wf.name)

# Or manage lifecycle manually
client = TorcClient()
await client.ping()
# ... do work ...
await client.close()

Set the server URL via environment variable to avoid hardcoding:

export TORCPY_API_URL=http://localhost:8080/torcpy/v1

import os
from torcpy.client import TorcClient

url = os.environ.get("TORCPY_API_URL", "http://localhost:8080/torcpy/v1")
async with TorcClient(url) as client:
    ...

docs.title: Create and Run a Workflow from a Spec File
docs.text:
import asyncio
from torcpy.client import TorcClient, WorkflowSpec, create_workflow_from_spec
from torcpy.client.job_runner import JobRunner, JobRunnerConfig

async def main():
    async with TorcClient() as client:
        # Load and create workflow
        spec = WorkflowSpec.from_file("pipeline.yaml")
        wf_id = await create_workflow_from_spec(client, spec)
        print(f"Created workflow {wf_id}")

        # Run locally
        config = JobRunnerConfig(
            poll_interval=2.0,
            output_dir="output/",
            max_parallel_jobs=0,   # 0 = unlimited (resource-constrained)
        )
        runner = JobRunner(client, wf_id, config)
        stats = await runner.run()
        print(f"Done: {stats}")

asyncio.run(main())

docs.title: Create a Workflow Programmatically
docs.text:
from torcpy.client import TorcClient
from torcpy.models import (
    WorkflowCreate, JobCreate, FileCreate, ResourceRequirementsCreate
)
from torcpy.models.enums import JobStatus

async with TorcClient() as client:
    # 1. Create workflow
    wf = await client.create_workflow(
        WorkflowCreate(name="my-pipeline", user="alice")
    )
    wf_id = wf.id

    # 2. Create files
    f_in = await client.create_file(wf_id, FileCreate(
        workflow_id=wf_id, name="input", path="data/in.csv", st_mtime=1710000000.0
    ))
    f_out = await client.create_file(wf_id, FileCreate(
        workflow_id=wf_id, name="output", path="data/out.csv"
    ))

    # 3. Create resource requirements
    rr = await client.create_resource_requirements(wf_id, ResourceRequirementsCreate(
        workflow_id=wf_id, num_cpus=4, memory="8g", runtime="PT1H"
    ))

    # 4. Create jobs
    j1 = await client.create_job(wf_id, JobCreate(
        workflow_id=wf_id,
        name="process",
        command="python process.py",
        input_file_ids=[f_in.id],
        output_file_ids=[f_out.id],
        resource_requirements_id=rr.id,
    ))

    j2 = await client.create_job(wf_id, JobCreate(
        workflow_id=wf_id,
        name="report",
        command="python report.py",
        depends_on_job_ids=[j1.id],
    ))

    # 5. Initialize and run
    await client.initialize_workflow(wf_id)

docs.title: Claim and Execute Jobs Manually
docs.text:
For custom job runners:

from torcpy.client import TorcClient
from torcpy.models.enums import JobStatus

async with TorcClient() as client:
    while True:
        jobs = await client.claim_next_jobs(workflow_id=1, count=5)
        if not jobs:
            await asyncio.sleep(2)
            continue

        for job in jobs:
            # Execute the job's command somehow
            result = run_subprocess(job.command)

            # Record result
            await client.create_result(1, ResultCreate(
                workflow_id=1,
                job_id=job.id,
                return_code=result.returncode,
                exec_time_minutes=result.duration / 60,
            ))

            # Complete it
            status = JobStatus.COMPLETED if result.returncode == 0 else JobStatus.FAILED
            await client.complete_job(1, job.id, status=status)

Or set the log level in Python:

import logging

docs.title: Server SQL Debug Logging
docs.text:
To see every SQL query the server executes, set the aiosqlite logger:

import logging
logging.getLogger("aiosqlite").setLevel(logging.DEBUG)

Or run the server with debug logging:

PYTHONPATH=src python -c "
import logging, uvicorn
logging.basicConfig(level=logging.DEBUG)
from torcpy.server.app import create_app
uvicorn.run(create_app(), log_level='debug')
"

docs.title: Inspect the Database Directly
docs.text:
The SQLite database is a standard file you can inspect with any SQLite tool:

sqlite3 torcpy.db

# List all workflows
SELECT id, name, user FROM workflow;

# Count jobs by status
SELECT status, COUNT(*) FROM job WHERE workflow_id=1 GROUP BY status;

# Find stuck pending jobs
SELECT id, name, status FROM job WHERE status=3 AND workflow_id=1;

# Check unblocking queue
SELECT id, name FROM job WHERE status IN (5,6,7,8) AND unblocking_processed=0;

docs.title: Jobs stuck in pending
docs.text:
A job is pending when it has been claimed by a worker but not yet completed. This can happen if a worker crashes mid-execution.

Fix: Reset stuck pending jobs:

from torcpy.models.enums import JobStatus
from torcpy.models.job import JobUpdate

result = await client.list_jobs(workflow_id, status=int(JobStatus.PENDING))
for job in result["items"]:
    await client.update_job(workflow_id, job["id"], JobUpdate(status=JobStatus.READY))

docs.title: Blocked jobs never becoming ready
docs.text:
Check:

  1. Are their dependencies actually completing?
  2. Is the background unblock task running? (check server logs)
  3. Are there circular dependencies? (all jobs in the cycle stay blocked)
# Check the unblocking queue in SQLite
sqlite3 torcpy.db "
SELECT j.id, j.name, j.status, j.unblocking_processed
FROM job j
WHERE j.workflow_id = 1 AND j.status IN (5,6,7,8)
  AND j.unblocking_processed = 0;
"

docs.title: "Database is locked" errors
docs.text:
SQLite is optimized for single-writer, many-reader usage. If you see lock errors:

  • Ensure you're not running multiple server processes against the same DB file.
  • The server uses WAL mode and busy-timeout (5s) to handle concurrent access.

docs.title: Worker exits immediately
docs.text:
Check the server is running and reachable:

torcpy --url http://localhost:8080/torcpy/v1 server run  # should already be running
curl http://localhost:8080/torcpy/v1/ping

docs.title: Job Logs
docs.text: TorcPy captures stdout and stderr for each job as files on the worker's filesystem.

docs.title: Default Log Location
docs.text:
By default, logs are written to the output/ directory in the current working directory:

output/
  job_1.stdout
  job_1.stderr
  job_2.stdout
  job_2.stderr
  ...

docs.title: Custom Output Directory
docs.text:
torcpy workflows run 42 --output-dir /scratch/my_logs
torcpy run pipeline.yaml --output-dir /scratch/my_logs

docs.title: Log File Naming
docs.text:
Files are named job_{id}.stdout and job_{id}.stderr. Get the job ID from:

torcpy jobs list 42

docs.title: Stdio Modes
docs.text:
Control what is captured by setting stdio_mode in the runner config:

Mode Files created separate (default) job_N.stdout + job_N.stderr combined job_N.log (stdout + stderr merged) no_stdout job_N.stderr only no_stderr job_N.stdout only none No files — output discarded

In Python:

from torcpy.client.job_runner import JobRunnerConfig
from torcpy.models.enums import StdioMode

config = JobRunnerConfig(
    output_dir="/scratch/logs",
    stdio_mode=StdioMode.COMBINED,
)

docs.title: Viewing Logs
docs.text:
# View stderr for job 47
cat output/job_47.stderr

# Follow stdout in real time (if job is running)
tail -f output/job_47.stdout

# Search across all logs
grep -r "Error" output/
grep -r "OOMKill" output/

docs.title: Summary Report
docs.text:
Shows job counts grouped by status and total execution time:

torcpy reports summary <workflow_id>

Workflow 42 Summary
  Total jobs: 101
  completed: 99
  failed: 2
  canceled: 0

  Total execution time: 312.4 minutes
  Failed results: 2

docs.title: JSON Output
docs.text:
All reports support --format json for scripting:

torcpy reports results 42 --format json > results.json
python -c "
import json
data = json.load(open('results.json'))
failed = [r for r in data if r['return_code'] != 0]
print(f'Failed: {len(failed)}')
"

docs.title: Python Client
docs.text:
from torcpy.client import TorcClient

async with TorcClient() as client:
    status = await client.workflow_status(workflow_id=42)
    results = await client.list_results(workflow_id=42)

    total_time = sum(
        r.get("exec_time_minutes", 0) or 0
        for r in results["items"]
    )
    print(f"Total CPU-minutes: {total_time:.1f}")

docs.title: REST API Reference
docs.text: All endpoints are under the base path /torcpy/v1.

docs.title: Conventions
docs.text:
• Request body: JSON with Content-Type: application/json
  • Pagination: All list endpoints accept ?offset=0&limit=10000 (max 10,000)
  • Response format: JSON. Lists return {"items": [...], "offset": 0, "limit": N, "has_more": false}
  • Status codes: 201 Created, 204 No Content, 404 Not Found, 422 Validation Error
